description:
You are acting as a platform engineer who has just finished writing three Kubernetes
manifests for an application.  
To safeguard those manifests you must create a *deterministic* backup and
write a machine-readable report that the CI system will later inspect.

Existing materials  
1. Directory that holds the manifests:  
   • /home/user/k8s-manifests/deployment.yaml  
   • /home/user/k8s-manifests/service.yaml  
   • /home/user/k8s-manifests/configmap.yaml  
2. A destination directory does **not** exist yet.

Final state that must be reached
1. A new directory called /home/user/k8s-backup **must exist**.
2. Inside it there must be a compressed archive named
   /home/user/k8s-backup/k8s_manifests_backup.tar.gz
   which contains every *.yaml file that is located in
   /home/user/k8s-manifests at the moment the task starts.
   • The archive must store the files **without any parent directories**
     (i.e. tar -C /home/user/k8s-manifests . … style).
3. Also inside /home/user/k8s-backup there must be a log file
   /home/user/k8s-backup/backup.log whose exact contents follow this format:

   ARCHIVE_PATH=/home/user/k8s-backup/k8s_manifests_backup.tar.gz  
   ARCHIVE_SHA256=<64-character lowercase hex hash of the archive>  
   FILE_COUNT=<number of *.yaml files inside the archive>  

   • The three lines must appear **in that order** with no
     additional lines or trailing whitespace.  
   • FILE_COUNT must equal the amount of YAML files archived
     (which is 3 for the initial state).  
   • ARCHIVE_SHA256 must be the SHA-256 digest of the **binary archive file**,
     not of its contents listing.

4. The manifests themselves must remain untouched in their original location.

Compliance notes  
• The archive file name, the directory names and the log file path are **fixed
  and must match exactly** so that the automated checker can locate them.  
• The checker will:  
  a) read the three log-file variables,  
  b) verify that the reported path exists,  
  c) recompute the SHA-256 digest of the archive and compare it to
     ARCHIVE_SHA256,  
  d) list the archive, ensure it contains exactly FILE_COUNT entries,
     and that each of the three expected filenames is present.

Typical workflow (do NOT output):
1. Create destination directory.  
2. Create the archive with tar & gzip.  
3. Compute SHA-256 of the produced archive.  
4. Produce backup.log following the strict three-line format.

If everything is done correctly, the CI script will pass.
truth:
Pre-existing filesystem objects before the agent starts:

Directory /home/user/k8s-manifests with three files.

1. /home/user/k8s-manifests/deployment.yaml  
Content (LF line endings):
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: nginx-deployment
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: nginx
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: nginx
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx:1.14.2
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80

2. /home/user/k8s-manifests/service.yaml  
Content:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: nginx-service
spec:
  selector:
    app: nginx
  ports:
  - protocol: TCP
    port: 80
    targetPort: 80
  type: ClusterIP

3. /home/user/k8s-manifests/configmap.yaml  
Content:
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: nginx-config
data:
  nginx.conf: |
    events {}
    http {
      server {
        listen 80;
        location / {
          return 200 'Hello, Kubernetes!';
        }
      }
    }

No /home/user/k8s-backup directory exists yet.  
The test harness will execute the steps described in the public section to
check the archive and log file.  
It will accept any valid SHA-256 value that matches the produced archive, so no
hard-coded digest is required here.
